run-llama/llama_index#18698.body:
### Bug Description

Unlike `AzureOpenAI` LLM class which correctly loads credentials from environment variables, the `AzureOpenAIEmbedding` class does not properly retrieve the API key from the `AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY` environment variable. This causes authentication errors when trying to use Azure OpenAI embeddings without explicitly passing the API key.

- llama-index-embeddings-azure-openai version: 0.3.2
- Python version: 3.12
- OS: WSL2

Both initialization methods should work since the API key is properly set in the environment variables, just as it does with the `AzureOpenAI` LLM class.

The issue stems from the `resolve_openai_credentials` function in OpenAI utilities.

Two key issues:
1. It looks for `"OPENAI_API_KEY"` instead of `"AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY"`
2. It returns an empty string `""` rather than `None` when no key is found, causing subsequent conditional checks like `if self.api_key is None` to fail

Meanwhile, `AzureOpenAI` (LLM) has additional logic in its `_get_credential_kwargs` method to check for `AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY`:

```python
import os
self.api_key = self.api_key or os.getenv("AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY")
```

A fix could be updating the `_get_credential_kwargs()` method in `AzureOpenAIEmbedding` to use the correct environment variable:

```python
def _get_credential_kwargs(self, is_async: bool = False) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    if self.use_azure_ad:
        self._azure_ad_token = refresh_openai_azuread_token(self._azure_ad_token)
        self.api_key = self._azure_ad_token.token
    else:
        import os
        self.api_key = self.api_key or os.getenv("AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY")
        
    if not self.api_key:  # Check for empty string or None
        raise ValueError(
            "You must set an `api_key` parameter. "
            "Alternatively, you can set the AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY env var OR set `use_azure_ad=True`."
        )
        
    return {
        "api_key": self.api_key,
        "azure_ad_token_provider": self.azure_ad_token_provider,
        "azure_endpoint": self.azure_endpoint,
        "azure_deployment": self.azure_deployment,
        "base_url": self.api_base,
        "api_version": self.api_version,
        "default_headers": self.default_headers,
        "http_client": self._async_http_client if is_async else self._http_client,
    }
```

Alternatively, fix the `resolve_openai_credentials` function to correctly handle Azure-specific environment variables.

What do you think ?

### Version

llama-index-embeddings-azure-openai==0.3.2 llama-index-embeddings-openai==0.3.1

### Steps to Reproduce

First, set environment variables:
```
AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT=https://your-resource.openai.azure.com
AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY=your-api-key
OPENAI_API_VERSION=2024-09-01-preview
```

Here a minimal reproduction script:
```python
from llama_index.embeddings.azure_openai import AzureOpenAIEmbedding

# This works fine (explicitly passing API key)
embedder_working = AzureOpenAIEmbedding(
    # should be automatically set from env
    api_key=os.environ["AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY"],
    # automatically set from env
    # azure_endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT"],
    # api_version=os.environ["OPENAI_API_VERSION"],
)
    
response = embedder_working.get_text_embedding("hello")
    
# This fails (relying on environment variable)
embedder_failing = AzureOpenAIEmbedding()
    
response = embedder_failing.get_text_embedding("hello")
```

The second initialization will fail with an authentication error.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#18698.title: [Bug]: AzureOpenAIEmbedding fails to properly load API key from environment variables

run-llama/llama_index#18723.body:
### Feature Description

I think it would be nice to draw the workflow as a Mermaid diagram, to embed it in documentation that can be rendered on GitHub.  

### Reason

The HTML is very useful locally, but I'm aiming for auto-generated documentation, where the CI/CD process can run a script to keep all workflow diagrams updated.

### Value of Feature

Once a large project chooses llamaindex-workflow to manage its workflows, it can be difficult to follow what each workflow does and how it works.

Having this kind of documentation can help developers (or even those without development knowledge) understand more easily and quickly what each workflow does.
run-llama/llama_index#18723.title: [Feature Request]: Draw workflow as mermaid diagram

run-llama/llama_index#18733.body:
### Bug Description

Version 0.12.35 recently introduced the `AgentWorkflowStartEvent` in this [PR](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/pull/18628/files). This start event is passed in to the `run` function using the argument `start_event`. In cases where the user is passing in kwargs to the `run` function, an error message occurs: 

`Keyword arguments are not supported when 'run()' is invoked with the 'start_event' parameter.`

This error comes from [here](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/9bb6d037f50df14d93bb5a7d36dc5bca7b7e7b75/llama-index-core/llama_index/core/workflow/workflow.py#L311). 

This did not occur in versions 0.12.34 and prior because no `start_event` was passed in. My team's code broke because we were invoking the agent workflow with `kwargs`, but suddenly when the code updated to 0.12.35, those kwargs are being ignored. Our workflows need to take kwargs as we pass additional context that is not captured by the chat history and user message. Until kwargs are supported, we have pinned the version of llama-index to 0.12.34. 

### Version

0.12.35

### Steps to Reproduce

Run any agent workflow with some kwargs.
run-llama/llama_index#18733.title: [Bug]: Agent Workflow Cannot Take Kwargs

run-llama/llama_index#18752.body:
### Feature Description

Allowing the delegation of httpx client **kwargs from BasicMCPClient via sse_client() and stdio_client() down to create_mcp_http_client() in _httpx_util.py allows much more flexibility in how transfer towards an FastMCP server can be established.



### Reason

A simple example that bothered me today and required me to monkeypatch the creation of the underlying httpx.AsyncClient creation is the following.

- Creating a simple FastMCP server in my local network that uses spotipy to control my spotify playlist
- Using the Spotify API requires you to configure a secure (i.e. no http but https) redirect URI
- Now I did not want to overcomplicate things and created a self-signed certificate for my server and ran it vis https.
- The Problem: The llamaindex BasicMCPClient does not offer any option to ignore/accept self-signed certificates and only inside create_mcp_http_client, the required dict of httpx client **kwargs is being created.

This is a non-productive sample of course but I wanted to play around with my agents to see if I can make them controlling my running spotifiy instance.

### Value of Feature

It just allows you to configure the httpx AsyncClient on the leveal where you instantiate the llamaindex MCPClient. That makes things like dealing with self-signed certificates way easier and avoid monkeypatching inside the llamaindex code.
run-llama/llama_index#18752.title: [Feature Request]: Add **kwargs to create_mcp_http_client()

run-llama/llama_index#18753.body:
### Bug Description

I'm attempting to get docs from a folder in a sharepoint site using the SharepointReader

I'm creating the reader object as follows:
`        sharepoint_reader = SharePointReader(
            client_id=client_id,
            client_secret=client_secret,
            tenant_id=tenant_id,
            sharepoint_site_id=full_sharepoint_site_id,
            sharepoint_folder_id=folder_id,
            drive_id=drive_id,
            sharepoint_site_name=sharepoint_site_name
        )`

I get the error listed in the title.

The source code for _get_drive_id starts as follows:
`        if hasattr(self, "_drive_id"):
            return self._drive_id

        if self.drive_id:
            return self.drive_id

        self._drive_id_endpoint = f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/sites/{self._site_id_with_host_name}/drives"
`

If the user sets drive_id on init, then the function returns and self._drive_id_endpoint never gets set.

But several methods try to use self._drive_id_endpoint which then causes the error (e.g. _get_sharepoint_folder_id, _get_permissions_info, _list_folder_contents, _list_drive_contents)

### Version

0.12.36

### Steps to Reproduce

Try to run sharepoint_reader.list_resources(...) after instantiating object with drive_id included.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```

run-llama/llama_index#18758.body:
### Bug Description

Hi, 
This is a follow-up issue to  [issue #18057](https://github.com/jerryjliu/llama_index/issues/18057) , which describes a bug where using a `Context` object in `ToolSpec`  causes a Pydantic schema generation error. 


#### Problem

Although `FunctionTool` is designed to handle functions with `ctx: Context` as the first parameter (by removing it from the schema and expecting it at runtime), `BaseToolSpec.get_fn_schema_from_fn_name` does **not** ignore the `ctx` parameter when generating the schema. This causes Pydantic to attempt to generate a schema for the `Context` type, resulting in an error.

#### Suggested Fix

Update `BaseToolSpec` to detect and ignore the `ctx` parameter (as in `FunctionTool.from _defaults`) when calling `create _schema_from_function`


### Version

0.12.27

### Steps to Reproduce


```python
from llama_index.core.tools.tool_spec.base import BaseToolSpec
from llama_index.core.workflow import Context

class MyToolSpec(BaseToolSpec):
    spec_functions = ["sum"]

    async def sum(self, ctx: Context, a: int, b: int) -> int:
        return a + b

if __name__ == "__main__":
    tool_spec = MyToolSpec()
    tool_spec.to_tool_list() 
```

run-llama/llama_index#18760.body:
### Bug Description

When the model sends two consecutive tool calls, they are being erroneously merged into a single tool call where the keyword arguments (tool name, arguments, etc) are the concatenation of the arguments of the two calls.

E.g.: Consider the variable `content` in the function `astream_chat` of `llms/bedrock_converse/base.py`. Suppose that, at a given moment, it has the value:
```
{'text': "I will get the current temperature and humidity values.", 'toolUseId': 'tooluse_jHxvnHFzSui0djQzx0DT1A', 'name': 'get_temperature', 'toolUse': {'input': '{"sensor_id": "0"}', 'toolUseId': 'tooluse_jHxvnHFzSui0djQzx0DT1A', 'name': 'get_temperature'}}
```
And, we receive the following `chunk` from the LLM:
```
{'contentBlockStart': {'start': {'toolUse': {'toolUseId': 'tooluse_zup8IcxPQ2-xPv-5sg0XxA', 'name': 'get_humidity'}}, 'contentBlockIndex': 2}}
```
When this `chunk` is merged into the `content` (by calling `join_two_dicts`), the `content` becomes:
```
{'text': "I will get the current temperature and humidity values.", 'toolUseId': 'tooluse_jHxvnHFzSui0djQzx0DT1Atooluse_zup8IcxPQ2-xPv-5sg0XxA', 'name': 'get_temperatureget_humidity', 'toolUse': {'input':  '{"sensor_id": "0"}', 'toolUseId': 'tooluse_jHxvnHFzSui0djQzx0DT1A', 'name': 'get_temperature'}}
```
Note that the fields `toolUseId` and `name` have been erroenously merged. The same will happen when the LLM eventually sends the `input` for the second function call, which will eventually lead to a `JSONDecodeError`.

### Version

llama-index==0.12.36, llama-index-llms-bedrock-converse==0.5.1

### Steps to Reproduce

Define an `AgentWorkflow` with a single `FunctionAgent` and at least two tools. Use the `BedrockConverse` client (with Claude Sonnet 3.5, for instance). Create a prompt that leads the LLM to make two consecutive tool calls.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#18760.title: [Bug]: BedrockConverse is not merging tool calls correctly in `stream_chat` and `astream_chat`.

run-llama/llama_index#18767.body:
### Question Validation

- [x] I have searched both the documentation and discord for an answer.

### Question

Hi, I'm trying to understand the code of multi-agent, I found that in the handoff function the context sets which agent to call next (via `next_agent` property). However, in the [code](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/f5a68816c0cbccc4dc6b2bc3d2ee69c667671093/llama-index-core/llama_index/core/agent/workflow/multi_agent_workflow.py#L502), we get this value and do not reset it, it turns out that the next agent will call itself by default if the handoff is not called, which is normal. However, if we reuse the context the next time the workflow is called, we will get that the next agent will already be defined if the handoff was not called.
run-llama/llama_index#18767.title: [Question]: Is it normal that "next_agent" is never reset?

from llama_index.core.llms import ChatMessage, DocumentBlock, MessageRole, TextBlock

msg = ChatMessage(role=MessageRole.USER, blocks=[DocumentBlock(path="Cushman.pdf"), TextBlock(text="What does this file contain?")])

print(response.message.blocks[0].text)

run-llama/llama_index#18797.body:
Hello,

### Feature Description

It would be highly beneficial to support the creation of `PostgresKVStore` and `PostgresDocumentStore` instances using existing SQLAlchemy `Engine` and/or `AsyncEngine` objects. This would enable developers to reuse preconfigured connection pools, apply custom engine settings, and integrate more seamlessly with applications that already manage their own database connections.

### Proposed Usage:

```python
engine = create_engine(...)
async_engine = create_async_engine(...)

kv_store = PostgresKVStore(engine=engine, async_engine=async_engine, table_name="kv_store")
doc_store = PostgresDocumentStore(postgres_kvstore=kv_store)
```

### Reason

Currently, both `PostgresKVStore` and its wrapper `PostgresDocumentStore` only support two factory methods — `from_uri(...)` and `from_params(...)` — for instantiation. 

This design forces every consumer to recreate their own connection pools and gives developers no way to inject or share an existing SQLAlchemy `Engine` or `AsyncEngine`. 

As a result, you lose out on centralized pool management, custom connection settings, and any middleware or event hooks you’ve already configured on your engines.

### Value of Feature

Allowing `PostgresKVStore` and `PostgresDocumentStore` to be created from existing SQLAlchemy `Engine` and/or `AsyncEngine` instances provides greater flexibility and control for developers. 

It would enable efficient reuse of connection pools, seamless integration with existing database infrastructure, and better alignment with application-level configurations.
run-llama/llama_index#18797.title: [Feature Request]: Create PostgresKVStore and PostgresDocumentStore from existing SQLAlchemy Engine

run-llama/llama_index#18804.body:
### Bug Description

For some background, I'm using LlamaIndex with the `OpensearchVectorStore`/`OpensearchVectorClient`, as well as `VectorStoreIndex`.

Some version information:
```
llama-index                              0.12.29
llama-index-core                         0.12.30
llama-index-vector-stores-opensearch     0.5.2
```

I've spent the last few days trying to figure out why the `metadata` field of each `BaseNode` included in a `VectorStoreQueryResult` was seemingly incorrect/unexpected each time. Moreso, I noticed that the `metadata` field contained a nested `metadata` key, vector embeddings returned from the OpenSearch query, and so on.

I first noticed this due to the _huge_ amount of data feeding into the LLM (mostly caused by the embedding data being added as context for each source node; possibly related to #17003 , though I've confirmed the embeddings are _not_ in the metadata but are rather being returned from the query itself). After that, I inspected this further via my own logging in the `query` function:

https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/56f3c2f22bf27a301303284a6fc83cb343287af1/llama-index-integrations/vector_stores/llama-index-vector-stores-opensearch/llama_index/vector_stores/opensearch/base.py#L796-L803

From there, I noticed that `query` returns the result from `self._to_query_result(res)`, thus indicating that this was the "problem area".

https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/56f3c2f22bf27a301303284a6fc83cb343287af1/llama-index-integrations/vector_stores/llama-index-vector-stores-opensearch/llama_index/vector_stores/opensearch/base.py#L833

Upon inspecting `_to_query_result` and realizing that **my nodes were being parsed through the `except` branch of the logic** (given this [comment](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/56f3c2f22bf27a301303284a6fc83cb343287af1/llama-index-integrations/vector_stores/llama-index-vector-stores-opensearch/llama_index/vector_stores/opensearch/base.py#L888), I suppose they are legacy nodes), I realized that **BOTH the `metadata` AND `extra_info` fields were being set when initializing the `TextNode`**:

https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/56f3c2f22bf27a301303284a6fc83cb343287af1/llama-index-integrations/vector_stores/llama-index-vector-stores-opensearch/llama_index/vector_stores/opensearch/base.py#L897-L904

Looking at the "definition" of `extra_info`, it becomes immediately clear that this is a _deprecated_ field, where the _setter_ sets `self.metadata` and the _getter_ simply returns `self.metadata`:

https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/56f3c2f22bf27a301303284a6fc83cb343287af1/llama-index-core/llama_index/core/schema.py#L437-L451

Now, my question is **why do we set `metadata` AND `extra_info` when initializing `TextNode` in this case?** With the parsing, `metadata` is first "set" with derived `metadata` (pulled from source), and _then_ `extra_info` is "set" with the value of `source` itself, thus overriding the actual, expected metadata.

I'm trying to understand if this behavior is expected, or if this is a bug. Thanks!

### Version

0.12.29

### Steps to Reproduce

Simply run the below script and observe that the printed metadata is the value set in `extra_info`, not `metadata`:

```python
from llama_index.core.schema import TextNode

v = TextNode(
    text="Hello World",
    metadata={"document_name": "My New Document!"},
    embedding=[0.000123, 0.3123123123, 0.3141441234123],
    extra_info={"foo": "This is unexpected!"}
)

print(v.metadata)
```
```
{'foo': 'This is unexpected!'}
```

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#18804.title: [Bug]: BaseNode/TextNode metadata unexpectedly overridden by extra_info field

run-llama/llama_index#18840.body:
### Feature Description

In addition to the `rows_retrievers`, the `NLSQLRetriever` class should support an optional `cols_retrievers` or `values_retrievers` parameter (the most relevant name for this parameter can be discussed). This allows retrieving relevant values for individual columns instead of entire rows, providing more efficient contexts for text-to-SQL.

Example:

```python
# Create database, column retrievers, and LLM...
...

# Define column-specific retrievers for each table
cols_retrievers = {
    "city": {
        "name": city_name_retriever,
        "country": city_country_retriever,
    }
}

# Initialize the text-to-SQL retriever
text_to_sql_retriever = NLSQLRetriever(
    sql_database=database,
    tables=["city"],
    llm=_llm,
    cols_retrievers=cols_retrievers,
)
```

This would result in the following context being generated:

> Table 'city' has columns: name (VARCHAR(64)), country (VARCHAR(64)), .
> Here are some relevant values of text columns:
> name: Paris, New-York, London
> country: Germany, Spain, China

One important consideration when implementing this feature is the potential increase in token usage and latency at query time. A naive implementation might pass the query to all column retrievers, each independently embedding it for retrieval, resulting in redundant computation and unnecessary overhead. To mitigate this, the feature should optimize query routing—for example, by introducing an optional embedding parameter in the NLSQLRetriever class. If provided, the query embedding could be computed once and reused across all column retrievers, reducing both token usage and latency.

### Reason

Currently, the `NLSQLRetriever` does not support fine-grained retrieval at the column level for SQL tables. It primarily operates at the row level via rows_retrievers. 
This means the context passed to the language model often includes entire rows, even when only a few columns are semantically relevant to the user query. This can dilute the relevance of the context and introduce unnecessary noise, especially for wide tables.

In addition, some columns contain categorical values (e.g., country, status, department) that are either highly repetitive across rows or rarely retrieved when using row-level embeddings.
 As a result, relevant column values can be entirely missed, especially when they don't co-occur with other highly ranked row features. 
Indexing columns individually would reduce token overhead during ingestion, eliminate redundancy, and improve the retrieval of relevant values independently of other columns' content.


### Value of Feature

Column-level retrieval should enable more focused and efficient context generation by retrieving only the most relevant values for specific columns instead of entire rows. 
It should reduce token usage during data ingestion, improve precision, and ensure better coverage of categorical values that are often repetitive or missed in row-level retrieval. 
It should also enhance accuracy in wide or complex schemas and support modular, column-specific retrieval strategies, leading to more reliable text-to-SQL generation.
run-llama/llama_index#18840.title: [Feature Request]: Add Columns Retrievers in NLSQLRetriever

At this point in the video, the Context object is restored from a dict after the workflow has been interrupted by an `InputRequiredEvent` and the workflow is resumed using the `HumanResponseEvent`:

run-llama/llama_index#18849.body:
### Feature Description

To reduce redundant embedding computations, the `BaseEmbedding` class could be extended to accept an optional `BaseKVStore` parameter for embedding caching. This enhancement addresses concerns raised in issues #18840 and #18843, particularly regarding repeated query embedding computations in `NLSQLRetriever` and potentially other retriever classes.

Integrating this feature is straightforward: the query embedding methods — `get_query_embedding` and `aget_query_embedding` — can first check the cache before performing embedding computation.

### Reason

In certain cases—such as in the `NLSQLRetriever` class—multiple vector store retrievers are invoked. Each retriever processes the same query independently, leading to redundant embedding computations. This results in unnecessary computational and token overhead, which in turn increases retrieval latency and overall cost.

### Value of Feature

Implementing a caching mechanism in the `BaseEmbedding` class offers a simple and generic solution to eliminate redundant embedding computations, effectively reducing query latency and cost.
run-llama/llama_index#18849.title: [Feature Request]: Add Embedding Caching

Add `rows_retrievers` and `cols_retrievers` parameters to the  `NLSQLTableQueryEngine` class and forward them to  `NLSQLRetriever`.

The `NLSQLTableQueryEngine` class is missing the `NLSQLRetriever`'s `rows_retrievers` and `cols_retrievers` parameters, forcing the user to manually build a `RetrieverQueryEngine` from a `NLSQLRetriever` object to use them.

### Bug Description

Incorrect token counting for Google GenAI models when using `complete` call.

### Version

0.12.37

### Steps to Reproduce

- Create custom callback manager and add token counting handler with verbosity mode on
- Trigger `complete` with a prompt
- Notice how the handler logs 2 different token counts

```python
import tiktoken
from llama_index.llms.google_genai import GoogleGenAI
from llama_index.core.callbacks import CallbackManager, TokenCountingHandler

handler = TokenCountingHandler(
            tokenizer=tiktoken.encoding_for_model('gpt-4o').encode,  # Fallback tokenizer
            verbose=True,
        )

llm = GoogleGenAI(
    model='gemini-1.5-flash',
    api_key='API_KEY_HERE',
    callback_manager=CallbackManager([handler]),
)

llm.complete('Answer with "Hello, World" in German')

print(handler.total_llm_token_count)  # Should be 13 found 32
print(handler.prompt_llm_token_count)  # Should be 9 found 22
print(handler.completion_llm_token_count)  # Should be 4 found 10
```

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell
LLM Prompt Token Usage: 13
LLM Completion Token Usage: 6
LLM Prompt Token Usage: 9
LLM Completion Token Usage: 4
32
22
10
```
run-llama/llama_index#18875.title: [Bug]: Incorrect token counting for Google GenAI

run-llama/llama_index#18899.body:
### Feature Description

>  [Multi-language Analyzers](https://milvus.io/docs/multi-language-analyzers.md)
> When Milvus performs text analysis, it typically applies a single analyzer across an entire text field in a collection. If that analyzer is optimized for English, it struggles with the very different tokenization and stemming rules required by other languages, such as Chinese, Spanish, or French, resulting a lower recall rate. For instance, a search for the Spanish word “teléfono” (meaning “phone”) would trip up an English‑focused analyzer: it may drop the accent and apply no Spanish‑specific stemming, causing relevant results to be overlooked.

```
class BM25BuiltInFunction(BaseMilvusBuiltInFunction):
    """BM25 function built in Milvus."""

    def __init__(
        self,
        input_field_names: Union[str, List[str]] = DEFAULT_TEXT_KEY,
        output_field_names: Union[str, List[str]] = DEFAULT_SPARSE_EMBEDDING_KEY,
        function_name: str = None,
        function_description: str = "",
        function_params: Optional[Dict] = None,
        analyzer_params: Optional[Dict[Any, Any]] = None,             
       ** multi_analyzer_params: Optional[Dict[Any, Any]] = None,  **  # add this variables
        enable_match: bool = False,
    ):
        if not function_name:
            function_name = f"bm25_function_{str(uuid.uuid4())[:8]}"
        super().__init__(
            function_name,
            FunctionType.BM25,
            input_field_names,
            output_field_names,
            function_description,
            function_params,
        )
        self.analyzer_params = analyzer_params
        self.enable_match = enable_match
        **self.multi_analyzer_params = multi_analyzer_params**

    def get_field_kwargs(self) -> dict:
        field_schema_kwargs: Dict[Any, Any] = {
            "enable_analyzer": True,
            "enable_match": self.enable_match,
        }
        if self.analyzer_params is not None:
            field_schema_kwargs["analyzer_params"] = self.analyzer_params

        **if self.multi_analyzer_params is not None:
            field_schema_kwargs["multi_analyzer_params"] = self.multi_analyzer_params**
        return field_schema_kwargs
```

Sorry, Im the newer in rag, Im not quite sure that code will produce the correct effect, but it can work

### Reason

BM25BuiltInFunction is not have `multi_analyzer_params`

### Value of Feature

> When Milvus performs text analysis, it typically applies a single analyzer across an entire text field in a collection. If that analyzer is optimized for English, it struggles with the very different tokenization and stemming rules required by other languages, such as Chinese, Spanish, or French, resulting a lower recall rate. For instance, a search for the Spanish word “teléfono” (meaning “phone”) would trip up an English‑focused analyzer: it may drop the accent and apply no Spanish‑specific stemming, causing relevant results to be overlooked.
run-llama/llama_index#18899.title: [Feature Request]: Milvus 2.5.12 for Multi-language Analyzers

run-llama/llama_index#18978.body:
### Bug Description

Hello,

When using LlamaIndex with Qdrant as the vector store, the current implementation for generating node_ids (for SQL Table Schema) uses [`str(hash(table_identity))`](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/03b5dc9fab40bbac8a8624c578db97fab748d006/llama-index-core/llama_index/core/objects/table_node_mapping.py#L63). This can sometimes produce negative integers or unsigned integers as strings. But Qdrant expects point IDs to be either unsigned integers or UUID strings. If the id is not in the expected format, Qdrant returns a format error such as:

```
Format error in JSON body: value -39672 ... is not a valid point ID, valid values are either an unsigned integer or uuid ...
```

**Suggested Fix:**  
Replace the use of [`str(hash(table_identity))`](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/03b5dc9fab40bbac8a8624c578db97fab748d006/llama-index-core/llama_index/core/objects/table_node_mapping.py#L63) with a Qdrant-compatible UUID string, for example:
```python
import uuid
table_identity = f"{obj.table_name}{obj.context_str}"
deterministic_id = str(uuid.uuid5(uuid.NAMESPACE_DNS, table_identity))
```
This ensures IDs are always valid UUID strings and accepted by Qdrant.

### Version

0.12.38

### Steps to Reproduce


1. Use LlamaIndex with Qdrant and ingest a SQL table schema node.
2. If the generated hash is negative or an unsigned integer in string format, Qdrant will reject the request.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell
Format error in JSON body: value -39672 ... is not a valid point ID, valid values are either an unsigned integer or uuid ...
```
run-llama/llama_index#18978.title: [Bug]: TableNode Ids use Python hash() - Sometimes producing Unsigned Integers

run-llama/llama_index#18988.body:
### Feature Description

apoc.meta.data() by default will scan the entire graph. This is fine for smaller or more token/instructional size graphs of say under 1M nodes and relationships. But when you have large graph of half a billion nodes, it would be nice to call this function with the sample argument, such as apoc.meta.data({sample:100}).

This will need to be changed/implemented in:
llama-index-integrations/graph_stores/llama-index-graph-stores-neo4j/llama_index/graph_stores/neo4j/base.py
llama-index-integrations/graph_stores/llama-index-graph-stores-neo4j/llama_index/graph_stores/neo4j/neo4j_property_graph.py

So I suggest changing the default to 100, ask for user input, or base it upon the size of the graph.

### Reason

Since apoc.meta.data() cannot be run with parallel execution, it will basically lock up the database for several hours until completed. 
If user input is prompted, the user can always enter "-1" to ignore the sampling.
Refer to https://neo4j.com/docs/apoc/current/overview/apoc.meta/apoc.meta.data/#_config_parameters for more guidance.

### Value of Feature

_No response_
run-llama/llama_index#18988.title: [Feature Request]: Change the apoc.meta.data() calls to account for large graphs

run-llama/llama_index#19050.body:
### Bug Description

The [VectorMemoryBlock](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/main/llama-index-core/llama_index/core/memory/memory_blocks/vector.py) is retrieving memories from a vector store that are pertinent for replying to the user. 

The problem is that this memory block [only receives in input the chat history](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/5fd1041f50a63b49bd6de3f0a00a366d5566c53e/llama-index-core/llama_index/core/memory/memory.py#L561), which does not contain the last user prompt. Hence the relevancy of the memories that are being returned is questionable. In fact, the last user prompt should be the most important signal for retrieving pertinent memories from the vector store.

### Version

0.12.41

### Steps to Reproduce

Example of memories in the vector store

M1) User likes Chinese food
M2) User loves his cat

Example of a conversation that leads to unexpected memories being returned:

U1) I'm looking for a good restaurant, any suggestion?
A1) ... (cannot retrieve memories from vector store, since chat history is empty)
U2) I'm looking to buy a toy for my pet
A2) ... (retrieves only M1 since chat history only contains U1 and A1, not useful in the context of answering to U2)

and so on

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#19050.title: [Bug]: VectorMemoryBlocks can't retrieve memories from last user prompt

### Bug Description

For a list of ChatMessage input to OpenAIResponses LLM, only the last item's text survives ignoring all the other inputs.

### Version

llama-index-core==0.12.42, llama-index-llms-openai==0.4.5

### Steps to Reproduce

With `llama-index-llms-openai` version later than 0.3.44,

```python
from llama_index.core.llms import ChatMessage
  from llama_index.llms.openai.responses import OpenAIResponses
  llm = OpenAIResponses("gpt-4o-mini")
  messages = [
      ChatMessage(
          role="system", content="You are a pirate with a colorful personality"
      ),
      ChatMessage(role="user", content="What is your name"),
  ]
  resp = llm.chat(messages)
  print(resp)
```

Ahoy there! Ye can call me Captain Jollybeard, the most colorful pirate to sail the seven seas! What treasure of knowledge be ye seekin’, matey? 🏴‍☠️✨

run-llama/llama_index#19108.body:
### Bug Description

I'm currently evaluating the use of Mistral Medium and spotted a few issues:
1. `llama-index-multi-modal-llms-mistralai` has a constraint on `llama-index-llms-mistralai>=0.4.0,<0.5"`
2. Image URL of type `pydantic.networks.AnyUrl` causes ValidationError
3. Azure AI Foundry API: Base64 encoded images are required to be packed into a `mistralai.models.imageurl.ImageURL`. I recon that this should be fixed on the side of Azure, as it makes little sense to have this structure:
```
{
    'image_url': {
         'url': 'data:image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgo...',
    },
    'type': 'image_url'
}
```
instead of
```
{
    'image_url': 'data:image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgo...',
    'type': 'image_url'
}
```
but nonetheless, it prevents us from accessing Azure deployments and both formats are supported by Mistrals Python SDK.

I might create a PR for these, but I'd appreciate some feedback on how you'd proceed with the third issue.

### Version

0.12.42

### Steps to Reproduce

```
from llama_index.multi_modal_llms.mistralai import MistralAIMultiModal
from llama_index.core.schema import ImageNode
import logging
logging.basicConfig()
logging.getLogger().setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

api_key = ""  # API key for mistral or alternative deployment
api_endpoint= None  # Only set if using an alternative deployment, e.g. Azure

mistralai_mm_llm = MistralAIMultiModal(
    model="mistral-medium-2505", api_key=api_key, endpoint=api_endpoint,
)


# Case 1: Providing a URL
image_document = ImageNode(image_url="https://tripfixers.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/11/eiffel-tower-with-snow.jpeg")
# Case 2: Providing a path (which the image will be loaded from and passed as B64 encoded string)
image_document = ImageNode(image_path=r"eiffel-tower-with-snow.jpeg")


complete_response = mistralai_mm_llm.complete(
    prompt="Describe the image as an alternative text in a few words",
    image_documents=[image_document],
)

print(complete_response)

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```

run-llama/llama_index#19116.body:
### Bug Description

The MilvusVectorStore instantiates both a MilvusClient and AsyncMilvusClient in the __init__. The MilvusClient accepts a kwarg with "alias" to name and reuse the synchronous connection to the Milvus server, but the AsyncMilvusClient does not and instead uses the "alias" positional parameter with the name "using" which causes the kwarg["alias"] to give the Connections.connect() method the "alias" parameter twice.

Until the AsyncMilvusClient either fixes this or adds support for naming the connection with "alias" we need to remove the "alias" key from the kwargs being used by it (but keep it for the MilvusClient since it is a valid parameter.

So the __init__ of MilvusVectorStore should contain this:

        kwargs.pop("alias", None)   # <-- added until AsyncMilvusClient supports 'alias'
        self._async_milvusclient = AsyncMilvusClient(
            uri=uri,
            token=token,
            **kwargs,  # pass additional arguments such as server_pem_path
        )

### Version

llama-index 0.12.42   -  llama-index-vector-stores-milvus 0.5.0

### Steps to Reproduce

1) Instantiate a MilvusVectorStore with the "alias" paraemter

        vector_store = MilvusVectorStore(
            uri="http://0.0.0.0:15930",
            db_name="db",
            user="user",
            password="password",
            collection_name="collection_name",
            alias="<IM_THE_CAUSE_OF_THE_ERROR",
        )

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell
TypeError("Connections.connect() got multiple values for argument 'alias'")
```
run-llama/llama_index#19116.title: [Bug]: Passing "alias" to MilvusVectorStore constructor throws error: Connections.connect() got multiple values for argument 'alias'

run-llama/llama_index#19125.body:
### Bug Description

tests/test_llms_ollama.py::test_chat_with_think: failed
tests/test_llms_ollama.py::test_async_chat_with_think: skipped

There are few issues with these two tests which try to verify if thinking mode work as expected:

1. They call chat() with think set to false: `response = llm.chat([ChatMessage(role="user", content="Hello!")], think=False)`. This woud cause the eventual http request with think set to 'None'. From the response message, the 'think' field is None, but the model would embed think message inside the response message. It should call chat with think set to True.
2. The tests try to get the thinking response by parsing key 'think', but the response would use 'thinking'
3. The async test miss the '@pytest.mark.asyncio' decorator, which caused test skipped.

By simply fixing the above issues, tests would pass.



### Version

0.12.42

### Steps to Reproduce

Set env:
export OLLAMA_THINKING_TEST_MODEL="qwen3:0.6b"

Go to folder:
cd llama-index-integrations/llms/llama-index-llms-ollama

Run tests:
uv run -- pytest tests/test_llms_ollama.py::test_chat_with_think -sv
uv run -- pytest tests/test_llms_ollama.py::test_async_chat_with_think -sv

### Bug Description

When using the Chain-of-Abstraction agent for simple test cases I am observing that placeholders are not replaced prior to attempting to parse the task as JSON, resulting in invalid JSON.

The generated Chain of Abstraction appears to be syntactically correct:
```
==== Generated Chain of Abstraction ====
To find the product of 123.123 and 101.101, I will first multiply these two numbers together, resulting
in [FUNC multiply(123.123, 101.101) = y1]. Then, I will take the product obtained and multiply it by 
12345, resulting in [FUNC multiply(y1, 12345) = y2]. The final result will be y2.
```

This appears to be an exact duplicate of https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/issues/16296

run-llama/llama_index#19152.body:
### Bug Description

When using the QdrantVectorStore in LlamaIndex, applying a metadata filter with FilterOperator.IN does not return results if the metadata values are stored as integers. 

The filter implementation converts all filter values to strings before passing them to Qdrant, while the ingestion pipeline stores metadata values as their original types (e.g., integers) and does not convert them to strings. This results in a type mismatch between the filter values (strings) and the stored metadata (integers), causing the filter to fail to match any records, even though a direct equality filter (FilterOperator.EQ) works as expected. 

The documentation and code do not provide a rationale for this conversion or a way to configure type handling in the filter logic ([QdrantVectorStore](https://docs.llamaindex.ai/en/latest/api_reference/storage/vector_store/qdrant/#llama_index.vector_stores.qdrant.QdrantVectorStore)).

### Version

0.12.43


### Steps to Reproduce

1. **Ingest nodes with integer metadata** (e.g., `some_id: 3`) using the LlamaIndex ingestion pipeline. The pipeline stores metadata as integers and does not convert them to strings.
2. **Query the Qdrant vector store** using a metadata filter with `FilterOperator.IN` and a list of integers (e.g., `[3, 4, 5]`).
3. **Observe that no results are returned**, even though a node with `some_id: 3` exists and can be found using `FilterOperator.EQ`.

---

### Code Example

```python
from llama_index.core.schema import TextNode
from llama_index.vector_stores.qdrant import QdrantVectorStore
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, StorageContext
from llama_index.core.vector_stores import MetadataFilter, MetadataFilters, FilterOperator
import qdrant_client

# Step 1: Ingest nodes with integer metadata
nodes = [
    TextNode(
        text="Example Node",
        metadata={"some_id": 3},  # Integer metadata
    ),
]

client = qdrant_client.QdrantClient(location=":memory:")
vector_store = QdrantVectorStore(client=client, collection_name="test_collection")
storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(vector_store=vector_store)
index = VectorStoreIndex(nodes, storage_context=storage_context)

# Step 2: Query with FilterOperator.IN
filters = MetadataFilters(
    filters=[
        MetadataFilter(key="some_id", value=[3, 4, 5], operator=FilterOperator.IN),
    ]
)
retriever = index.as_retriever(filters=filters)
results = retriever.retrieve("Example query")

# Step 3: Observe that results is empty, even though a node with some_id=3 exists
print("Results with IN filter:", results)

# Step 4: Try the same thing with EQ and observe we are able to return the node
filters_eq = MetadataFilters(
    filters=[
        MetadataFilter(key="some_id", value=3, operator=FilterOperator.EQ),
    ]
)
retriever = index.as_retriever(filters=filters_eq)
results_eq = retriever.retrieve("Example query")

print("Results with EQ filter:", results_eq)
```
## Outputs:
```cmd
Results with IN filter: []
Results with EQ filter: [NodeWithScore(node=TextNode(id_='fceedc4e-d48c-4871-afd9-b125816c7d75', embedding=None, metadata={'some_id': 3}, excluded_embed_metadata_keys=[], excluded_llm_metadata_keys=[], relationships={}, metadata_template='{key}: {value}', metadata_separator='\n', text='Example Node', mimetype='text/plain', start_char_idx=None, end_char_idx=None, metadata_seperator='\n', text_template='{metadata_str}\n\n{content}'), score=0.828115460741842)]
```
run-llama/llama_index#19152.title: [Bug]: QdrantVectorStore: Metadata filter with FilterOperator.IN converts filter values to str, causing type mismatch with integer metadata

run-llama/llama_index#19166.body:
### Bug Description

Good day, 

We have recently discovered the following bug in the llm anthroptic class when trying to make use the bedrock provider.

The input parameters seem to have changed either in llama index or by anthroptic. 

We get the following error 
```
TypeError: AnthropicBedrock.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'aws_access_key_id'
```

This is due to the input parameters changing to use `aws_access_key` and `aws_secret_key` over the current ones being used `aws_access_key_id` and `aws_secret_access_key` 

Please see portion of requirments.txt file and versions

run-llama/llama_index#19181.body:
### Feature Description

The `llama-index-vector-stores-pinecone` module is currently limited to Python versions `>=3.9,<3.13`. 

It will be great to have this opened upto <4.0 like most of the other vector-stores packages, from my (admittedly limited) understanding this should create no issues.

### Reason

Python 3.13 has been out for a while now.

This package is currently dependent on `pinecone>=7.0.0,<8.0.0` and it seems like the Pinecone Python SDK supports Python3.13 since its v6.0 released back in Feb: https://docs.pinecone.io/assistant-release-notes/2025#february-2025

The only other dependency here is `llama-index-core>=0.12.0,<0.13` which also supports upto <4.0, and plenty of other packages in this project dependent on the same allow upto <4.0.

### Value of Feature

This will allow projects using this package to be able to upgrade to Python3.13. I am aware of at least one project that I am working on where this package is the only reason we are still at v3.12. 

**Happy to open a PR for this!** And if this has not yet been upgraded for a good reason, please correct me 😄
run-llama/llama_index#19181.title: [Feature Request]: Update llama-index-vector-stores-pinecone to support Python 3.13

### Question Validation

### Question

In order to achieve Code Generation and execution feature for my `RagWorkflow`, I have been using the CodeAct agent provided by Llama Index here: https://docs.llamaindex.ai/en/stable/examples/agent/code_act_agent/

- I wanted to add some custom citation related prompts to the CodeAct agent.
- But as there are two different prompts available here: https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/main/llama-index-core/llama_index/core/agent/workflow/codeact_agent.py#L82-L113
- `code_act_system prompt` and `system_prompt`. I ended up adding the `system_prompt` and that had no effect and then later found out this `code_act_system prompt` is the actual prompt used in the code generation.
- The mention of different purpose and use of these two prompts would be helpful
run-llama/llama_index#19184.title: [Question]: Use of `system_prompt` parameter in `CodeAct` Agent

run-llama/llama_index#19185.body:
### Bug Description

- System prompts sent via `ChatPromptTemplate` and `ChatMessage` are not being passed as a system instruction while using the `achat` or `astructured_predict` method.
- This [documentation of Google GenAI](https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/learn/prompts/system-instructions) says that system instruction must be sent via the `GenerateContentConfig` `system_instruction` just like the following example:

```python
from google import genai
from google.genai.types import GenerateContentConfig, HttpOptions

client = genai.Client(http_options=HttpOptions(api_version="v1"))
response = client.models.generate_content(
    model="gemini-2.5-flash",
    contents="Why is the sky blue?",
    config=GenerateContentConfig(
        system_instruction=[
            "You're a language translator.",
            "Your mission is to translate text in English to French.",
        ]
    ),
)
print(response.text)
# Example response:
# Pourquoi le ciel est-il bleu ?
```

**Case: 1**
- While using GoogleGenAI class provided by Llamaindex `from llama_index.llms.google_genai import GoogleGenAI`, when in between multiple chats there is a `ChatMessage` like as folllows, the system message is being treated as a usual message rather than a system instruction:
```python
from llama_index.llms.google_genai import GoogleGenAI
from llama_index.core.llms.types import ChatMessage, MessageRole

# This doesn't work properly - system message treated as regular message
llm = GoogleGenAI(model="gemini-2.0-flash-exp", api_key="your_key")

messages = [
    ChatMessage(
        role=MessageRole.SYSTEM,
        content="You are a professional French translator."
    ),
    ChatMessage(
        role=MessageRole.USER,
        content="Translate 'Hello' to French"
    )
]

response = await llm.achat(messages)  # System instruction not properly set
```

**Case: 2**
- ChatPromptTemplate Equivalent with `astructured_predict`. Here also the system message is treated as a simple message instead of system prompt.

```python
from llama_index.core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from llama_index.llms.google_genai import GoogleGenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class AnalysisOutput(BaseModel):
    analysis: str = Field(description="Text analysis")
    recommendations: list[str] = Field(description="Recommendations")

llm = GoogleGenAI(model="gemini-2.0-flash-exp", api_key="your_key")

# System instruction won't work properly
chat_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate(
    message_templates=[
        ChatMessage(
            role=MessageRole.SYSTEM,
            content="You are a language expert. Provide detailed analysis."
        ),
        ChatMessage(
            role=MessageRole.USER,
            content="Analyze this text: '{text}'"
        )
    ]
)

response = await llm.astructured_predict(
    AnalysisOutput,
    chat_prompt,
    text="The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
)
```


### Version

0.12.28

### Steps to Reproduce

- Look into either of these scenarios in the bug description.
- Try to log the entire request and response sent to the LLM.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#19185.title: [Bug]: System prompts sent via `ChatPromptTemplate` and `ChatMessage` are being interpreted as user message in Google GenAI

run-llama/llama_index#19187.body:
### Bug Description

I was using a `ReActAgent` workflow when I received a response similar to the following:

```
To answer the question, I need to handoff to the research agent.

Action: handoff
Action Input: {"to_agent": "research", "reason": "**insert_any_reason_here**"}
```

Note the absence of `Thought:`, which is expected from the LLM in a ReAct agent.

Investigating a bit, I found out that the `ReActOutputParser` processes such a situation as follows:

```python
        if "Thought:" not in output:
            # NOTE: handle the case where the agent directly outputs the answer
            # instead of following the thought-answer format
            return ResponseReasoningStep(
                thought="(Implicit) I can answer without any more tools!",
                response=output,
                is_streaming=is_streaming,
            )

        # An "Action" should take priority over an "Answer"
        if "Action:" in output:
            return parse_action_reasoning_step(output)

        if "Answer:" in output:
            thought, answer = extract_final_response(output)
            return ResponseReasoningStep(
                thought=thought, response=answer, is_streaming=is_streaming
            )
```

which returns a `ResponseReasoningStep` object instead of a `ActionReasoningStep` needed to trigger function calling. Therefore, the Action function call is ignored and the whole response is treated as a final answer, including the Action and Action Inputs.

I think the first if statement should become:
```python
        if "Thought:" not in output and "Action:" not in output:
```
In addition, the `extract_tool_use` function should be updated to take into account the possibility of matching with no thought:
```python
def extract_tool_use(input_text: str) -> Tuple[str, str, str]:
    pattern = (
        r"(?:\s*Thought: (.*?)|(.+))\n+Action: ([^\n\(\) ]+)\n+Action Input: .*?(\{.*\})"
    )

    match = re.search(pattern, input_text, re.DOTALL)
    if not match:
        raise ValueError(f"Could not extract tool use from input text: {input_text}")

    thought = (match.group(1) or match.group(2)).strip()
    action = match.group(3).strip()
    action_input = match.group(4).strip()
    return thought, action, action_input
```


### Version

0.12.43

### Steps to Reproduce

I created a simple AgentWorkflow with a ReAct orchestrator agent and a ReAct research agent.
The issue occurs after several rounds of chat, probably due to the LLM context window filling up, making it ignore the system prompt specifying to always reply start with `Thought: `.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#19187.title: [Bug]: ReAct workflow ignores Action if no Thought is produced

### Bug Description

The first bug is that in the function `to_tool_list_async` , when the resource function is being created, `resource.uri` should be passed not `resource.name`. Here:

```
fn = self._create_resource_fn(resource.name)
```

Second issue is that the fetch resources should also consider the resource templates (dynamic resources which allow parameters to be filled in the uri). Right now, the fetch_resources method only calls the `client.list_resources` and not `client.list_resource_templates` which it should and construct the uri in run time.

Just create an MCP server with a resource. Set include_resources=True in llamaindex mcp client and connect to the server.

### Documentation Issue Description

On https://docs.llamaindex.ai/en/stable/examples/llm/azure_openai/#configure-environment-variables The document uses OPENAI_API_KEY as the environment variable for api key. However, the right way should use AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY
```python
import os

os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = "<your-api-key>"
os.environ[
    "AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT"
] = "https://<your-resource-name>.openai.azure.com/"
os.environ["OPENAI_API_VERSION"] = "2023-07-01-preview"
```

### Documentation Link

https://docs.llamaindex.ai/en/stable/examples/llm/azure_openai/#configure-environment-variables

run-llama/llama_index#19228.body:
### Bug Description

  ## Bug Description

  When using `Mem0Memory` with `AgentWorkflow` and attempting to save/restore workflow context using `Context.to_dict()` and `Context.from_dict()`, the
  deserialization fails with an error about instantiating abstract class `BaseMemory`.

  The issue occurs because `Mem0Memory` objects get serialized into the workflow context's `globals` section, but during deserialization, the system
  attempts to instantiate the abstract `BaseMemory` class instead of the concrete `Mem0Memory` implementation.

  ## Error Message
  WARNING: Failed to restore context, starting fresh: Failed to deserialize value for key memory: Can't instantiate abstract class BaseMemory with
  abstract methods from_defaults, get, get_all, put, reset, set

### Version

 - **LlamaIndex Core**: 0.12.31   - **LlamaIndex Memory Mem0**: 0.3.1   - **Python Version**: 3.11.12

### Steps to Reproduce

. Create an `AgentWorkflow` with `Mem0Memory`:

  ```python
  from llama_index.core.workflow import Context, JsonSerializer
  from llama_index.memory.mem0 import Mem0Memory
  from llama_index.core.agent.workflow import AgentWorkflow, FunctionAgent
  from pathlib import Path
  import json

  # Create Mem0Memory instance
  mem0_memory = Mem0Memory.from_client(
      context={"user_id": "test_user", "conversation_id": "test_conv"},
      search_msg_limit=10
  )

  # Create simple workflow
  agent = FunctionAgent(
      name="TestAgent",
      description="Test agent",
      system_prompt="You are a test agent",
      llm=your_llm,  # Your LLM instance
      tools=[],
  )

  workflow = AgentWorkflow(
      agents=[agent],
      root_agent=agent.name,
  )

  # First run - works fine
  ctx = Context(workflow)
  response = await workflow.run(user_msg="Hello", ctx=ctx, memory=mem0_memory)

  # Save context - this works
  serializer = JsonSerializer()
  context_data = ctx.to_dict(serializer=serializer)
  with open("test_context.json", "w") as f:
      json.dump(context_data, f)

  # Load context - this fails
  with open("test_context.json", "r") as f:
      context_data = json.load(f)

  # This line throws the error
  ctx_restored = Context.from_dict(workflow, context_data, serializer=serializer)
```
  2. Run the workflow once with memory
  3. Save the context using ctx.to_dict()
  4. Try to restore the context using Context.from_dict() - this fails

  Expected Behavior

  Context should serialize and deserialize correctly when using Mem0Memory objects, allowing for persistent workflow state across sessions.

  Actual Behavior

  Deserialization fails because the serialized Mem0Memory object in the context cannot be properly reconstructed, causing the system to attempt
  instantiation of the abstract BaseMemory class.

  Workaround

  Currently using context filtering to remove memory objects before serialization:

  # Before saving context
  context_data = ctx.to_dict(serializer=serializer)
  if "globals" in context_data and "memory" in context_data["globals"]:
      context_data["globals"].pop("memory", None)

  Additional Context

  - This issue appears to be specific to Mem0Memory - other memory implementations may not have the same serialization issues
  - The memory object gets embedded in the workflow context's globals section
  - Both JsonSerializer and JsonPickleSerializer exhibit the same behavior
  - The issue prevents proper stateful agent workflows that persist across sessions


### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#19228.title: [Bug]: Context serialization fails when using Mem0Memory: "Can't instantiate abstract class BaseMemory"

run-llama/llama_index#19240.body:
### Bug Description

I was using the `PGVectorStore`'s sparse search with `text_search_config="french" ` when I noticed some wrong results with the word "eglise" ("church", without an accent).
After debugging, I noticed that the `_build_sparse_query` method uses `ts_query` as follows:
```SQL
to_tsquery(replace(text(plainto_tsquery('french', 'eglise')), '&', '|'))
```
which produces `eg`

Running each part individually, we get:
```SQL
SELECT text(plainto_tsquery('french', 'eglise'));
> eglis

SELECT to_tsquery('eglis');
> eg
```
However, adding the search text config to `to_tsquery`, we get:
```SQL
SELECT to_tsquery('french', 'eglis');
> eglis
```

In short, the current use of `to_tsquery` uses the default text search configuration (english) instead of the specified `text_search_config`, leading to the stemming being applied twice with different languages.

### Version

llama-index-vector-stores-postgres==0.5.3

### Steps to Reproduce

Create a vector store with two documents: one with the word "eglise", one with the word "eg" and execute a sparse search.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#19240.title: [Bug]: Missing text search configuration in Postgres vector store sparse search query

run-llama/llama_index#19247.body:
### Bug Description

When using `LLM.astructured_predict` (or components that rely on it, like `SchemaLLMPathExtractor`) with an OpenAI model that supports parallel function calling (e.g., gpt-4.1-mini), a `TypeError` occurs that leads to silent data loss.

The LLM correctly identifies multiple distinct items in the text and returns a request for multiple, parallel tool calls. However, the LlamaIndex framework fails to process all of them. The root cause is a `TypeError` that happens when the internal `FunctionCallingLLM` logic tries to instantiate the dynamically-generated Pydantic output model. It incorrectly passes the tool's arguments as a single positional dict argument instead of using keyword arguments.
This `TypeError` breaks the internal loop that processes the list of tool calls requested by the LLM. As a result, only a subset of the tool calls are attempted (and fail), and the rest are dropped entirely. The end user receives an incomplete, partial result with no thrown exception, leading to silent data loss.

### Version

0.12.40

### Steps to Reproduce

```python
    def build_index(
        self, nodes: Sequence[BaseNode], show_progress: bool = True
    ) -> PropertyGraphIndex:
        """Builds a PropertyGraphIndex from the provided nodes."""
        if not self._formatted_extract_prompt:
            raise ValueError(
                "Extraction prompt has not been prepared. Call `prepare_prompts` first."
            )

        logger.info(f"Building PropertyGraphIndex from {len(nodes)} nodes...")

        self.index = PropertyGraphIndex(
            nodes=nodes,
            storage_context=self.storage_context,
            kg_extractors=self._get_kg_extractors(),
            embed_kg_nodes=True,
            show_progress=show_progress,
        )

        logger.info("PropertyGraphIndex built successfully.")

        if self.persist_dir:
            self.persist_graph_store()
        if self.graph_html_output_path:
            self.save_graph_visualization()

        return self.index

    def _get_kg_extractors(self) -> list[TransformComponent]:
        """Gets the extractors for building the knowledge graph."""
        implicit_extractor = ImplicitPathExtractor()
        schema_extractor = SchemaLLMPathExtractor(
            llm=Settings.llm,
            max_triplets_per_chunk=999,
            possible_entities=Entity,
            possible_relations=Relation,
            possible_entity_props=["description"],
            kg_validation_schema=validation,
            extract_prompt=self._formatted_extract_prompt,
            strict=True,
        )
        logger.info("Initialized KG extractors for product data")
        return [implicit_extractor, schema_extractor]
```
where `Entity & Relations` are `list[str]` & `validation` being a `list[Tuple(str, str, str)]`... like llama index expects... validation is mapping of entities to entities using relations.


when building index for large docs... each node can be of 4k tokens... (used `MarkdwonNodeParser` and `SentenceSplitter`).. so a response can also be of 4k tokens... then when using `OpenAI` models which support parallel function calling... the long response is split into parts and tool call is made...

then the first tool call always call with `args` which is not correct and throws `TypeError: get_function_tool.<locals>.model_fn() takes 0 positional arguments but 1 was given`

it should use `kwargs`. Though the llama-index retires and then `kwargs` were used... this process somehow is braking the rest of the tool calls. thus only the first call is reflecting.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/33103d50-40c3-4b53-8bee-0ebf0f771db6)

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/rishi/document_extraction/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/llama_index/core/instrumentation/dispatcher.py", line 369, in async_wrapper
    result = await func(*args, **kwargs)
             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/rishi/document_extraction/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/llama_index/core/tools/function_tool.py", line 294, in acall
    raw_output = await self._async_fn(*args, **all_kwargs)
                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/rishi/document_extraction/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/llama_index/core/tools/function_tool.py", line 22, in _async_wrapped_fn
    return await loop.run_in_executor(None, lambda: fn(*args, **kwargs))

run-llama/llama_index#19270.body:
### Feature Description

Ollama models now expose a `thinking` field in their streaming responses, providing insight into the model's intermediate reasoning steps. This was introduced in the [Ollama class here](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/1e3af99d01343718b302bd81b9394593268a89da/llama-index-integrations/llms/llama-index-llms-ollama/llama_index/llms/ollama/base.py#L147).  

I am requesting that the `FunctionAgent` (specifically in [`function_agent.py`](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/blob/1e3af99d01343718b302bd81b9394593268a89da/llama-index-core/llama_index/core/agent/workflow/function_agent.py#L63-L71)) and the `AgentStream` class be updated to include the `thinking` field from the LLM response in the streamed events. This would allow downstream application and UI to display the model's "thinking" process in real time.

```python
ctx.write_event_to_stream(
    AgentStream(
        delta=last_chat_response.delta or "",
        response=last_chat_response.message.content or "",
        tool_calls=tool_calls or [],
        raw=raw,
        current_agent_name=self.name,
        thinking_delta=last_chat_response.additional_kwargs.get("thinking_delta", ""),
    )
)
```

### Reason

Currently, the `AgentStream` object does not include or expose the `thinking` field, even though it is available in the Ollama LLM response (`last_chat_response.additional_kwargs["thinking_delta"]`). As a result, there is no way for downstream application to display this information. Since LlamaIndex is a framework supporting multiple LLM providers, and not all providers expose a `thinking` field, this field is not currently part of the standard agent streaming interface.


### Value of Feature

Exposing the `thinking` field in `AgentStream` would enable developers to build interactive UIs that can show the model's intermediate reasoning ("thinking") as it streams, improving transparency and user experience.
run-llama/llama_index#19270.title: [Feature Request]: Expose Ollama "thinking" in FunctionAgent's AgentStream

run-llama/llama_index#19290.body:
### Bug Description

When creating a `ChatResponse` object with a `ChatMessage` that has an empty string (`""`) as its `content`, calling `str(response)` returns `"system: None"` instead of `"system: "`.

This suggests that the `__str__` method is incorrectly treating an empty string as falsy and falling back to `None`, resulting in unexpected output.

### Version

0.12.42

### Steps to Reproduce

```python
from llama_index.core.llms.callbacks import ChatResponse
from llama_index.core.llms import ChatMessage

message = ChatMessage(content="", role="system")
response = ChatResponse(
    message=message,
    raw=None,
    delta=None,
    logprobs=None,
    additional_kwargs={}
)

print(str(response))  # Expected: "system: ", but got: "system: None"

```

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#19290.title: [Bug]: `ChatResponse.__str__` outputs `'None'` when content is an empty string

run-llama/llama_index#19336.body:
### Bug Description

When creating a `VectorStoreIndex` using `from_documents`, a provided `callback_manager` is not applied to the embedding model if the `embed_model` argument is omitted. This prevents callbacks, such as `TokenCountingHandler`, from tracking embedding token usage when relying on the global `Settings.embed_model`.

Note: A Pull Request addressing this issue has been submitted #19335 

### Version

0.12.47

### Steps to Reproduce

The following code demonstrates that embedding token counts are not recorded when relying on the default embed_model.

```python
from llama_index.core import (
    VectorStoreIndex,
    SimpleDirectoryReader,
    Settings,
)

from llama_index.core.callbacks import CallbackManager, TokenCountingHandler

from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding

token_counter = TokenCountingHandler()
callback_manager = CallbackManager([token_counter])

Settings.embed_model = OpenAIEmbedding(model="text-embedding-3-small")
documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./data").load_data()

token_counter.reset_counts()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    callback_manager=callback_manager
)

print(f"Embedding tokens used: {token_counter.total_embedding_token_count}")
assert token_counter.total_embedding_token_count == 0, "Bug confirmed: Embedding tokens were not counted."
print("Bug confirmed: Token counter was not triggered for embedding.\n")


token_counter.reset_counts()
index_fixed = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    embed_model=Settings.embed_model, # Explicitly pass the model
    callback_manager=callback_manager
)

print(f"Embedding tokens used: {token_counter.total_embedding_token_count}")
assert token_counter.total_embedding_token_count > 0, "Workaround failed."
print("Workaround successful: Token counter was triggered for embedding.")
```

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#19336.title: [Bug]: CallbackManager not applied to default embed_model in VectorStoreIndex.from_documents

run-llama/llama_index#19340.body:
### Bug Description

If the Milvus search returns documents that don't have _node_content set, a ValueError is thrown in `metadata_dict_to_node`, even if a text_key is configured. From my understanding, this behaviour was changed in https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/pull/18281 ([base.py line 1360 ff](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index/commit/061275e86655f496e7d90b44dd597b61beaca318#diff-a6388c5219c5ce3b511954b8abf4cd533379b8ef739910b2791993be39365297L1295-L1313)). Until then, a if the a text_key was configured, a TextNode was created directly, without calling `metadata_dict_to_node`.

I'm not sure what the ideal behaviour is, now that the text_key is set to a default value if it's not explicitly provided. I think it would make sense to first check if `_node_content` is set, and if not, create a TextNode directly.

### Version

llama_index 0.12.47, llama_index_vector_stores_milvus 0.8.5

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Insert documents into Milvus with only a field 'text' and a vector.
2. Create a MilvusVectorStore with `text_key` set to 'text'.
3. Attempt to search for documents.

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

run-llama/llama_index#19348.body:
### Bug Description

When I use the async functions of `llama-index-llms-azure-inference` with Azure AI Foundry models, the `aiohttp.client.ClientSession` does not seem to be closed properly and I get the following error:

```
Unclosed client session
client_session: <aiohttp.client.ClientSession object at 0x109fb94d0>
Unclosed connector
connections: ['deque([(<aiohttp.client_proto.ResponseHandler object at 0x10a1b2660>, 707937.664887041), (<aiohttp.client_proto.ResponseHandler object at 0x10a1b2820>, 707938.039953416)])']
connector: <aiohttp.connector.TCPConnector object at 0x10a1de310>
```

See also: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/13242

It could be fixed by changing the initialization to a lambda callable and using it as a context manager. If I find time, I might submit a PR for it as well.

```python
# Construct the client each time in order to close it afterwards again
self._async_client = lambda: ChatCompletionsClientAsync(
    endpoint=endpoint,
    credential=credential,
    user_agent="llamaindex",
    **client_kwargs,
)

# Later (instead of response = await self._async_client.complete(messages=messages, **all_kwargs)
async with self._async_client() as client:
    response = await client.complete(messages=messages, **all_kwargs)
```

### Version

llama-index-llms-azure-inference==0.4.0

### Steps to Reproduce

```python
import asyncio

from llama_index.llms.azure_inference import AzureAICompletionsModel

llm = AzureAICompletionsModel(
    model_name="Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8",
    credential="<API_KEY>",
    endpoint="<AZURE_ENDPOINT>",
    api_version="<AZURE_API_VERSION>",
)

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
results = loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.gather(*[llm.acomplete(prompt="Test prompt") for _ in range(2)]))
```

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell

```
run-llama/llama_index#19348.title: [Bug]: "Unclosed client session" in Azure AI Foundry async calls

run-llama/llama_index#19353.body:
### Bug Description

When calling `MockLLM.stream_complete("")` with `max_tokens=None`, the system attempts to construct a `LLMCompletionEndEvent` using a `None` response, which results in a `pydantic ValidationError`. However, passing an empty string "" to CompletionResponse is valid and expected behavior. The constructor accepts empty content without raising any errors:
```python
from llama_index.core.base.llms.types import CompletionResponse

response = CompletionResponse(text="", additional_kwargs={})
```

I think this issue is maybe caused by the same type-checking behavior as [Issue #19290](https://github.com/jerryjliu/llama_index/issues/19290), where empty strings (`""`) were treated as falsy and misinterpreted as `None`. But this specific instance was not fixed by [PR #19302](https://github.com/jerryjliu/llama_index/pull/19302), and still causes crashes at runtime.


### Version

llama-index-core==0.12.47

### Steps to Reproduce

```python
from llama_index.core.llms import MockLLM

llm = MockLLM(max_tokens=None)
response = llm.stream_complete("")
result = [chunk for chunk in response]

```

### Relevant Logs/Tracbacks

```shell
pydantic_core._pydantic_core.ValidationError: 1 validation error for LLMCompletionEndEvent
response
  Input should be a valid dictionary or instance of CompletionResponse [type=model_type, input_value=None, input_type=NoneType]
```
run-llama/llama_index#19353.title: [Bug]: `MockLLM.stream_complete` raises error on empty prompt when `max_tokens=None`

run-llama/llama_index#19360.body:
### Feature Description

I am requesting the addition of support for the `cached_content` feature in the `llama_index.llms.google_genai` module to enable context caching with Google's Gemini API. This feature would allow users to store and reuse input tokens for repetitive requests, reducing latency and costs when processing large or frequently referenced contexts, such as PDF documents or extensive system prompts.

The proposed integration would align with the existing Google GenAI SDK's context caching functionality, as demonstrated in the following example:

```python
from google import genai
from google.genai.types import CreateCachedContentConfig, Content, Part, HttpOptions
from llama_index.core.llms import ChatMessage
from llama_index.llms.google_genai import GoogleGenAI

# Initialize Google GenAI client for Vertex AI
client = genai.Client(
    http_options=HttpOptions(api_version="v1"),
    project="...",
    location="us-central1",
    vertexai=True
)

# Define content for caching (e.g., PDF files)
contents = [
    Content(
        role="user",
        parts=[
            Part.from_uri(
                file_uri="gs://cloud-samples-data/generative-ai/pdf/2312.11805v3.pdf",
                mime_type="application/pdf",
            ),
            Part.from_uri(
                file_uri="gs://cloud-samples-data/generative-ai/pdf/2403.05530.pdf",
                mime_type="application/pdf",
            ),
        ],
    )
]

model = "gemini-2.0-flash-001"

# Create cache
cache = client.caches.create(
    model=model,
    config=CreateCachedContentConfig(
        display_name='OS Book',
        system_instruction=(
            'You are an expert PDF analyzer, and your job is to answer '
            'the user\'s query based on the PDF file you have access to.'
        ),
        contents=contents,
        ttl="300s",
    )
)

# Initialize GoogleGenAI with cached_content
llm = GoogleGenAI(
    model=model,
    vertexai_config={"project": "...", "location": "us-central1"},
    cached_content=cache.name,
)

# Query using cached content
message = ChatMessage(role="user", content="Summarize the key points.")
response = llm.chat([message])
print(response)
```

**References:**  
- Google Gemini API Context Caching: https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/caching  
- Google Cloud Vertex AI Context Caching https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/context-cache/context-cache-overview

### Reason

Currently, the `GoogleGenAI` class in LlamaIndex does not support the `cached_content` parameter, resulting in an inability to leverage this feature. Adding support would involve updating the `GoogleGenAI` class to accept a `cached_content` parameter and pass it to the underlying Google GenAI SDK when initializing the model, similar to how `vertexai_config` is handled.

### Value of Feature

This feature would enhance the efficiency of LlamaIndex applications using Google’s Gemini models, particularly for use cases involving large documents or repetitive queries, and align with the cost-saving benefits highlighted in Google’s documentation.
run-llama/llama_index#19360.title: [Feature Request]: Add Support for Cached Content in llama_index.llms.google_genai

run-llama/llama_index#19363.body:
### Bug Description

When following example at https://docs.llamaindex.ai/en/stable/understanding/workflows/state/ I'm getting the error bellow:

```PydanticSchemaGenerationError: Unable to generate pydantic-core schema for <class '__main__.MyRandomObject'>. Set `arbitrary_types_allowed=True` in the model_config to ignore this error or implement `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` on your type to fully support it.```


### Version

0.12.47

### Steps to Reproduce

Code for reproduction
```
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, field_validator, field_serializer
from typing import Union


# This is a random object that we want to use in our state
class MyRandomObject:
    def __init__(self, name: str = "default"):
        self.name = name


# This is our state model
# NOTE: all fields must have defaults
class MyState(BaseModel):
    my_obj: MyRandomObject = Field(default_factory=MyRandomObject)
    some_key: str = Field(default="some_value")

    # This is optional, but can be useful if you want to control the serialization of your state!

    @field_serializer("my_obj", when_used="always")
    def serialize_my_obj(self, my_obj: MyRandomObject) -> str:
        return my_obj.name

    @field_validator("my_obj", mode="before")
    @classmethod
    def deserialize_my_obj(
        cls, v: Union[str, MyRandomObject]
    ) -> MyRandomObject:
        if isinstance(v, MyRandomObject):
            return v
        if isinstance(v, str):
            return MyRandomObject(v)

        raise ValueError(f"Invalid type for my_obj: {type(v)}")
```
